fragments.content: Drug tolerance is a diminished response to a drug following repeated administration, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect. Mechanisms include: pharmacokinetic tolerance (increased metabolism via enzyme induction - alcohol, barbiturates); pharmacodynamic tolerance (receptor downregulation/desensitization - opioids, benzodiazepines); tachyphylaxis (rapid tolerance after few doses - nitrates, amphetamines). Cross-tolerance occurs between drugs with similar mechanisms (benzodiazepines and alcohol both act on GABA receptors). Tolerance develops at different rates for different effects: opioid tolerance to analgesia and euphoria develops quickly; tolerance to constipation and miosis develops minimally. Reverse tolerance (sensitization) occurs with some drugs (alcohol neurotoxicity, stimulant psychosis).

Clinical management of tolerance: opioid rotation when tolerance limits analgesia (incomplete cross-tolerance allows dose reduction); drug holidays to restore sensitivity (nitrates require 10-12 hour nitrate-free interval); combination therapy to reduce individual drug tolerance development (cancer pain: opioids + NSAIDs + adjuvants); scheduled dosing rather than PRN to maintain steady levels and minimize tolerance. Benzodiazepine tolerance: limit duration to 2-4 weeks for anxiety/insomnia; if long-term needed, consider intermittent dosing or switch to non-benzodiazepine alternatives. Stimulant tolerance in ADHD: drug holidays may help but risk symptom recurrence; consider dose adjustment or alternative agents. Preventing tolerance: use lowest effective dose; avoid unnecessary dose escalation; address underlying causes rather than escalating symptomatic treatment.

Tolerance diagnosis based on: documented dose escalation without disease progression; diminished clinical effect despite adherence; withdrawal symptoms suggesting physical dependence (often accompanies tolerance). Differentiate from: disease progression (worsening condition requires higher doses); pseudoaddiction (undertreated pain seeking relief); hyperalgesia (opioid-induced increased pain sensitivity - paradoxical response requiring dose reduction not increase). Quantitative assessment: track dose-response relationship over time; calculate morphine milligram equivalents (MME) for opioids; monitor therapeutic indices. Experimental measures: receptor binding studies show decreased density/sensitivity; metabolic studies demonstrate increased clearance. Tolerance vs addiction: tolerance is expected physiologic adaptation; addiction involves compulsive use despite harm, craving, loss of control.
